source.title: Metabolic engineering and adaptive laboratory evolution of Kluyveromyces Marxianus for lactic acid production

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to engineer the non-conventional, acid-tolerant yeast Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid (LA) production under low-pH conditions, addressing limitations of lactic acid bacteria (LAB) and Saccharomyces cerevisiae. LAB requires expensive neutralization agents and complex nutrients; S. cerevisiae is Crabtree-positive and sensitive to low pH. K. marxianus is GRAS, fast-growing, acid-tolerant (pH 3–8), and can utilize lignocellulosic sugars. The hypothesis is that exploiting natural diversity, genetic engineering, and adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) can yield strains with high LA titers, yields, and tolerance, reducing neutralization needs and enabling use of cheap feedstocks.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Overview: PLA is a promising bioplastic from LA. Current LA production via LAB requires neutralization agents. This study explores K. marxianus for LA production. Screened 168 strains, selected 10 for engineering (pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration, cyb2Δ). Best strain Km3 further improved by ALE, reaching 120 g/L LA, yield 0.81 g/g, with less neutralization and xylose utilization. Genome sequencing identified SUA7 mutation causal for improved performance.
process_signals: Initial problem: LAB fermentation requires neutralization agents and complex nutrients.
Hypothesis: K. marxianus acid tolerance can reduce neutralization needs.
Approach: Screen diversity → engineer → evolve.
Key result: ALE increased LA production by 18%.
Causal mutation: SUA7.
methods: Genetic engineering: pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration, cyb2Δ
Adaptive laboratory evolution under LA stress
Whole genome sequencing for SNP identification
metrics: 18% increase in LA production after ALE

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: PLA production relies on LA, mostly from LAB fermentation, which requires neutralization agents (e.g., CaCO3) and complex nutrients. S. cerevisiae is a common cell factory but is Crabtree-positive (ethanol production under high sugar) and sensitive to low pH, making it suboptimal for LA. K. marxianus is an alternative: GRAS, fast growth (doubling time 0.75 h⁻¹), acid-tolerant (pH 3–8), metabolizes xylose/arabinose, and has a growing genetic toolbox. The study aims to exploit K. marxianus diversity and engineering for LA production.
process_signals: Problem: LAB neutralization and nutrient requirements limit sustainability.
S. cerevisiae limitations: Crabtree effect, low pH sensitivity.
K. marxianus advantages: acid tolerance, xylose utilization, GRAS.
Hypothesis: K. marxianus can be engineered for low-pH LA production.
failures_or_limits: S. cerevisiae not ideal for low-pH LA fermentations.

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: This section is empty in the provided text.

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: HPLC with Aminex HPX-87H column used to measure acid and sugar concentrations. Urea measured with test kit. Dry cell weight determined by centrifugation, washing, drying, and weighing.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is empty in the provided text.

section_gists.heading: High-throughput screening of 168 K. marxianus strains reveals large phenotypic diversity
section_gists.gist: Screened 168 strains on 19 conditions (stress, carbon sources, inhibitors). Large phenotypic diversity observed. Growth at pH 2.5 was possible but reduced; at 50 g/L LA, only two strains (Km3, Km4) grew. Selected 10 diverse, robust strains including benchmark NBRC 1777 for engineering.
process_signals: Screening rationale: identify robust chassis for LA production.
Key finding: only Km3 and Km4 grew at 50 g/L LA.
Selection criteria: overall robustness, diversity, LA tolerance.
Benchmark: NBRC 1777 included.
metrics: Growth at pH 2.5: 18% smaller colonies vs pH 3.5
failures_or_limits: Most strains failed to grow at 50 g/L LA, indicating LA toxicity is a major hurdle.

section_gists.heading: Genetic engineering enables LA production
section_gists.gist: Engineered 10 selected strains: pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration at PDC1 locus, cyb2Δ. pdc1Δ+LDH enabled LA production in all strains; cyb2Δ increased titers up to 2-fold. Best strain Km3^eng produced 14.8 g/L LA, yield 0.68 g/g in 16h flask with 2% glucose. Strain background strongly affected production (1.7–14.8 g/L). Acetic acid production varied; no glycerol detected.
process_signals: Engineering strategy: delete PDC1 to increase pyruvate, integrate LDH, delete CYB2 to prevent LA consumption.
cyb2Δ doubled LA titers.
Strain background crucial: Km3 best.
Byproduct: acetic acid still produced despite pdc1Δ.
methods: Shake flask assays in rich medium with 2% glucose
failures_or_limits: LA titers still low (max 14.8 g/L) and likely limited by LA toxicity.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution improved LA tolerance and production
section_gists.gist: ALE of Km3^eng under LA stress (no pH control) in three populations (A,B,C) plus control. LA concentration increased stepwise (15→30 g/L). After 52–104 generations, single colonies screened; all evolved strains grew at 30 g/L LA vs. parental only at 10 g/L. 13/24 selected evolved strains produced more LA. Whole genome sequencing of best clones: SNPs in SUA7, BCK1, etc. Reverse engineering: reverting SUA7 mutation reduced LA tolerance and production, confirming causality. SUA7^S279P mutation improved LA tolerance, production, and acetic acid tolerance. BCK1 mutation also beneficial. Combining SUA7* and BCK1* further improved growth in LA and acetic acid.
process_signals: Hypothesis: LA tolerance limits production; ALE can improve it.
ALE setup: increasing LA stress, no pH control, parallel populations.
Evolved strains gained ability to grow at 30 g/L LA.
SUA7 mutation causal: reversion reduced tolerance/production.
SUA7^S279P also improved acetic acid tolerance.
Combining SUA7* and BCK1* additive effect.
methods: ALE in medium with L-LA, stepwise increase (15→20→25→30 g/L)
Single colony isolation and screening in LA concentrations
Whole genome sequencing and SNP identification
Reverse engineering by reverting SNPs to wild type
metrics: Evolved strains grew at 30 g/L LA vs. parental 10 g/L
failures_or_limits: Not all evolved strains improved LA production (13/24).
Multiple SNPs per strain; only SUA7 and BCK1 validated.

section_gists.heading: Engineered strains reach industrially relevant LA titers in fed-batch fermentations
section_gists.gist: Fed-batch bioreactor fermentations (0.5 L, pH 6.0, DO>20%) with Km3^eng and SNP-introduced strains. Km3^eng reached 116 g/L LA, yield 0.83 g/g, productivity 1.39 g/L/h. Km3^eng,SUA7*: 120 g/L, yield 0.81 g/g, productivity 1.44 g/L/h, max productivity 3.43 g/L/h. Differences not statistically significant. Byproducts varied: SUA7* produced more acetic acid but less succinic acid. Low-pH fermentations: at pH 4.5, Km3^eng,SUA7* produced 94 g/L (yield 0.79 g/g); at pH 3.5, Km3^eng produced 54 g/L (yield 0.68 g/g).
process_signals: Validation in bioreactors at pH 6.0 (lower than industrial LAB pH 7.0).
SUA7* strain showed slightly higher LA titer and productivity.
Low-pH performance: still significant LA production at pH 4.5 and 3.5, but reduced.
Byproduct trade-offs: SUA7* increased acetic acid.
metrics: Km3^eng: 116 g/L, yield 0.83 g/g, prod 1.39 g/L/h
Km3^eng,SUA7*: 120 g/L, yield 0.81 g/g, prod 1.44 g/L/h
failures_or_limits: No statistically significant difference between strains at pH 6.0.
LA production decreased at lower pH.
Acetic acid byproduct increased in SUA7* strain.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Screening revealed diversity; agave isolates robust. Growth at pH 2.5 but not at 50 g/L LA suggests combined low pH and LA toxicity. Engineering: pdc1Δ abolished ethanol; cyb2Δ doubled LA. Acetic acid still produced via mitochondrial Ach1. ALE improved LA tolerance; SUA7^S279P mutation in transcription factor IIB enhanced LA tolerance and production, possibly via oxidative stress response. BCK1 mutation in cell wall integrity pathway also beneficial. Combining mutations additive. Low-pH fermentations show potential but need further improvement. Future: exploit xylose utilization for second-generation LA.
process_signals: Interpretation of screening: combined low pH and LA toxicity is key hurdle.
Acetic acid production explained by Ach1 activity.
SUA7 role: transcription factor IIB, may regulate oxidative stress genes.
BCK1 role: cell wall integrity, may improve stress tolerance.
Low-pH performance still suboptimal; further engineering needed.
Future direction: lignocellulosic feedstocks.
failures_or_limits: Only two mutations validated; other SNPs may contribute.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: K. marxianus is a promising LA producer. Combining biodiversity screening, genetic engineering, and ALE yielded strains with high LA production and stress resilience, enabling cheaper, more sustainable processes. Future work: leverage xylose metabolism for second-generation LA production.
process_signals: Success of integrated approach: screen → engineer → evolve.
Potential for lignocellulosic feedstocks.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study follows a logical progression: (1) Identify problem (LAB limitations) and propose K. marxianus as solution due to acid tolerance and xylose utilization. (2) Screen natural diversity to find robust chassis, revealing that LA toxicity is a major hurdle. (3) Engineer LA production pathway, but titers remain low due to LA toxicity. (4) Apply ALE to improve LA tolerance, identifying key mutations (SUA7, BCK1). (5) Validate in bioreactors, showing industrially relevant titers but with trade-offs (acetic acid). (6) Conclude that combined approach is effective and point to future improvements (low-pH, lignocellulose).
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the exact mechanism by which SUA7^S279P improves LA tolerance?
Are there other beneficial mutations in the evolved strains that were not validated?
Can further ALE or engineering eliminate acetic acid byproduct?
How to improve low-pH (≤4.5) LA production to avoid neutralization entirely?
What is the performance on real lignocellulosic hydrolysates?

ALE improved LA tolerance and production; SUA7 mutation identified as causal.

events.summary: Re-introducing wild type PMA1 improved LA tolerance and production, indicating negative effect of PMA1 nonsense mutation.
Hypothesis that heterozygous, nonsense PMA1 mutation emerged early, allowing survival and accumulation of beneficial mutations like SUA7 S279P.
Acetic acid production was higher in Km3^eng,SUA7* compared to parental strain, suggesting need for further engineering.
LA production by evolved K. marxianus is lower than highest reported LAB production, but offers advantages in nutrient and neutralization costs.
Further improvements in yield and productivity may be needed before industrial implementation; future efforts should optimize fermentation process.
Future efforts should focus on optimizing fermentation process, including separation of aerobic growth and anaerobic production phases.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains analysis of PMA1 mutation effect, hypothesis on its role, bioreactor validation of engineered strains, comparison with LAB, and future design suggestions.
This chunk contains only reference list entries and publisher's note. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: it requires complex and expensive feedstocks and large amounts of neutralization agents for pH control during fermentation.
as a Crabtree-positive yeast, S. cerevisiae tends to favor ethanol production under high sugar concentrations
it is still quite sensitive to extreme conditions encountered in some industrial processes, such as high temperatures
S. cerevisiae remains a sub-optimal choice for some specific applications where cells are subjected to low pH, as is the case in LA fermentations.
it is acid tolerant with a pH range for growth between 3 and 8
it can natively metabolize sugars present in lignocellulosic feedstocks, such as hemicellulose-derived xylose and arabinose
we combine different state-of-the-art engineering strategies and the underexplored natural K. marxianus biodiversity to obtain superior cell factories
we screen 168 different K. marxianus strains to identify 10 genetically distinct strains that show superior industrially-relevant phenotypes
we engineer each of the 10 strains to produce LA and further optimize production in the best candidates by using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and process tuning
For the deletion of PDC1 and CYB2, two 90 bp oligonucleotides were designed to be homologous to the regions flanking the genes
Genomic regions were targeted for engineering using a CRISPR/Cas9-gene editing tool for K. marxianus
Genome sequencing identified a mutation in the general transcription factor gene SUA7 that proved causal for the increased performance of the evolved clone.
To establish a high-throughput screening set-up, the strains were pinned in triplicates on solid medium
Table 2 High-throughput screening conditions
Pictures were taken by the robotic handling system HiTMan
The colony area (pixels) was determined using an in-house developed image analysis pipeline in MATLAB
To select the optimal candidate strains, the strains in the top 60 largest colony size for each of the tested conditions were listed.
The selected strains were at least for one condition in the top 60, ensuring a large phenotypic diversity in the strain selection.
Table 3 Criteria for strain selection
Three parallel cultures (A, B, C) were maintained with and one culture without L-LA addition (Control)
Similar to other studies [36, 37], no pH control was applied.
The ALE process was concluded once the population showed a sustained increase in growth rate compared to the starting population and individual strains with enhanced tolerance could be isolated.
Per population, 8 single colonies with the highest final cell density were picked for small-scale fermentations.
The best LA-producing isolate for each population was sent for whole genome sequencing.
The genomic DNA was isolated using the Genomic tip-20G kit from QIAGEN. Whole genome Illumina sequencing was performed at BGI Hongkong.
To revert the identified mutations to their wild type nucleotide, gDNAs were designed to specifically target the mutant nucleotide.
All genetic modifications were verified by Sanger sequencing (Eurofins Genomics).
The Infors HT Multifors 6×0.5 L system was used to evaluate the constructed strains further.
The pH was controlled at 6.0 (or 4.5 or 3.5) using 6 M NaOH (VWR), and temperature was maintained at 30 °C throughout the fermentation.
The mass balance was constructed according to the formulas in Figure S2, Supplemental File 2.
Any volume changes due to addition of base and feedstock, and removal of glucose and LA through sampling were considered to calculate the final LA yield and productivity.
Theoretical maximal yields for the different compounds were taken from Pentjuss et al. 2017 [26].
An initial high-throughput screening was performed on 168 K. marxianus strains isolated from diverse environments
in total 10 strains were selected for further engineering (Table 3), including 9 overall robust, diverse strains, and NBRC 1777, a commonly used K. marxianus chassis strain, as a benchmark.
LA production was enabled in the 10 selected strains by three genetic modifications introduced by CRISPR/Cas9-based genetic engineering
We hypothesized that LA tolerance was a limiting factor for LA production.
Three populations were evolved in medium supplemented with L-LA and without pH control (A, B, C), while one control population was evolved without LA addition (Control).
After 64, 81, 52 and 104 generations of evolution of populations A, B, C and Control, respectively
To identify mutations that influence LA tolerance, we focused on non-synonymous SNPs in protein-coding regions (Table 4) and reverted these SNPs to their wild type nucleotide in the evolved strains.
13 of the 24 strains produced more LA than the non-evolved strain.

the SUA7 mutation also improved tolerance against other organic acids, such as acetic acid
re-introducing the wild type PMA1 gene improved LA tolerance and production, indicating a negative effect of the PMA1 nonsense mutation on organic acid tolerance.

Our hypothesis is that the heterozygous, nonsense PMA1 mutation emerged early in population A. If this is a loss-of-function mutation, less protons would be pumped out of the cells, however, also ATP is conserved. This might have allowed the cells to survive the stress condition and accumulate other mutations, such as the SUA7 homozygous S279P mutation, that were beneficial for LA tolerance.

However, acetic acid production was higher in this strain when compared to the parental strain. Additional engineering, such as introducing the bacterial acetyl-CoA pathway, could be investigated to further improve performance.
the engineered K. marxianus strain offers an advantage regarding nutrient requirements and neutralization costs compared to the bacterial alternatives.

While the LA titers achieved in this study are among the highest reported for K. marxianus under aerobic conditions, further improvements in yield and productivity may be needed before the process can be implemented industrially.
Future efforts should focus on optimizing the fermentation process, including separation of an aerobic growth phase and anaerobic production phase.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Engineering Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid production under low-pH conditions to reduce neutralization costs and enable use of cheap feedstocks.

S. cerevisiae is suboptimal for lactic acid production due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.
K. marxianus is a promising alternative due to acid tolerance, fast growth, xylose utilization, and GRAS status.
Study design: screen 168 K. marxianus strains, engineer top 10 for lactic acid production, apply ALE, and validate in bioreactors.
High-throughput screening of 168 K. marxianus strains on 19 conditions reveals large phenotypic diversity; only two strains grew at 50 g/L lactic acid.

only two strains grew at 50 g/L LA

canonical_events.summary: Fed-batch bioreactor fermentations: Km3^{eng} reached 116 g/L lactic acid (yield 0.83 g/g); Km3^{eng},SUA7* reached 120 g/L (yield 0.81 g/g) at pH 6.0. Low-pH fermentations: at pH 4.5, 94 g/L; at pH 3.5, 54 g/L.
Not all evolved strains improved lactic acid production; only 13/24 showed increase. Acetic acid byproduct increased in SUA7* strain. Low-pH production still limited.
Lactic acid production by evolved K. marxianus is lower than highest reported LAB production, but offers advantages in nutrient and neutralization costs.

The limitations of S. cerevisiae motivate the hypothesis that K. marxianus could be a better host.
The hypothesis leads to the design of a study to exploit K. marxianus diversity and engineering.

Screening results enable selection of robust strains for engineering.
Selected strains are then genetically engineered for lactic acid production.

The hypothesis motivates the ALE experiment.
After ALE, sequencing and reverse engineering identify causal mutations.
Reverse engineering validates that SUA7 mutation improves tolerance and production.
Validated strains are tested in bioreactors for industrial relevance.
Bioreactor results show limitations: not all evolved strains improved, acetic acid increased, low-pH production still limited.
K13 notes limitations; K14 contextualizes them against LAB benchmarks, acknowledging trade-offs.

ALE with stepwise LA increase, evolved strains grow at 30 g/L
Whole genome sequencing and reverse engineering identify SUA7 S279P as causal
SUA7 mutation improves LA tolerance, production, and acetic acid tolerance
Fed-batch bioreactor: 120 g/L LA, yield 0.81 g/g at pH 6.0
Low-pH performance: 94 g/L at pH 4.5, 54 g/L at pH 3.5
Limitations: only 13/24 evolved strains improved, acetic acid byproduct, low-pH still suboptimal
Comparison to LAB: lower titers but cost advantages
